title: Fitting rubber webbing to a chair seat
subtitle: Stretch, interlace, and staple Pirelli webbing across the seat frame
excerpt: How to fit rubber elastic webbing as a seat-base support: spacing the runs, using a webbing stretcher to tension each strip, interlacing front-to-back and side-to-side, and stapling off.
sourceNotes: The Upholsterer's Step-by-Step Handbook (Dorling Kindersley). Collins Complete DIY Manual. Pirelli webbing fitting guidance (publicly available).

content.text: Pirelli rubber webbing

replaces both the spring layer and the jute webbing layer in modern chair construction. Strips run front-to-back and side-to-side, interlaced at each crossing. The correct tension is a 10 percent stretch: a 500 mm span is stapled at 550 mm measured flat. Using a
webbing stretcher
achieves the stretch consistently and leaves both hands free to staple.
Planning the layout

Space the strips at 50 mm apart (centre to centre), starting from the mid-point of the frame and working outward symmetrically. An odd number of strips keeps a strip at the centre. Mark the frame with a pencil line at each strip position before cutting.
Step 1, cut to length

Measure the inside span of the frame at each strip position. Multiply the span by 0.9 to get the cut length: this gives the 10 percent pre-stretch. Cut each strip with the upholstery shears; a square cut helps the staples seat evenly.
Step 2, staple the first end

Fold 15 mm of the webbing end over itself to form a double thickness. Place this fold on the top of the frame rail, centred on the pencil mark, and fire three 10 mm T50 staples across the width of the strip. The fold doubles the grip and prevents the staples tearing through.
Step 3, stretch and staple the second end

Hook the webbing stretcher peg through the free end of the strip. Press the stretcher against the opposite frame rail and lever down until the webbing is taut. Hold the stretcher in place, fold the free end over, and fire three staples across it as before. Release the stretcher: the webbing should spring slightly taut, not go slack.
Step 4, lay and interlace the cross strips

Fit all the front-to-back strips first. Then lay the side-to-side strips one at a time: weave each strip over and under alternate front-to-back strips before stretching and stapling. Alternate the over-under pattern with each successive cross strip so the weave is consistent across the seat.
What it looks like when it is right

When all strips are fitted, press the centre of the webbing with the flat of your hand: it should give 10 to 15 mm under firm pressure and return immediately. The surface should be level with no strips sitting obviously higher or lower than their neighbours.
Common mistakes
Tensioning by hand without a stretcher.
Hand tension is never sufficient and the webbing will sag within months under body weight.
Forgetting to fold the ends.
Staples through a single thickness of rubber will pull through. Always fold the end to double thickness before stapling.
Skipping the interlace.
Cross strips simply laid on top of front-to-back strips (not woven) will shift under load.
